data.title: Pricing The AGI, Top 3 Stocks For AI-Energy Alpha
data.description:
In my opinion, Wall Street consensus is currently mispricing the Artificial General Intelligence [AGI] revolution by treating it as a software and silicon scaling. What I see mostly in this is an ontological failure. To answer why? As the market enters 2026, the AGI build-out has fastly shifted into a thermodynamic and electrical physics asymptote. The market is assuming infinite compute elasticity, as I see Eaton's proprietary U.S. Dodge data center tracking that indicates a terminal physical bottleneck. The U.S. data center construction backlog is now at 206 Gigawatts [GWs] that indicates an 11-year duration at 2025 build rates.

But the industry technically cannot deploy 206 GWs of accelerated compute using legacy 480-volt AC architectures and traditional cooling without going beyond issues of entropy and grid capacity. The theme for the Picks is the Arbitrage of AI Energy Physics. To capitalize on the next decade of AGI shift, smart bulls should rotate from the silicon layer (my perspective) into the Top 3 stocks for AI Energy Alpha: Base-Load Generation (CEG stock), Voltage−Step Topology (ETN stock), and Entropy Management (VRT stock).

Constellation Energy Corporation Stock (CEG)

The main catalyst for Constellation Energy stock is the $22 billion Calpine acquisition and exclusive edge on generation. AGI training clusters are intolerant of grid intermittency. Meaning they need a 100% capacity factor. Constellation Energy, post the $22 billion acquisition of Calpine (January 2026), has changed into an irreplicable 55 GW energy apex asset (the largest private-sector power producer). I am focusing on the pricing-in of the volumetric synergy of this merger into CEG stock. By combining a 22 GW nuclear fleet operating at a 94.7% capacity factor + Calpine’s 23 GWs of dispatchable natural gas + geothermal (730 MW Geysers) + 800 MWs of battery storage, Constellation Energy has built a moat for hyperscaler Power Purchase Agreements [PPAs].

Moreso, the point of selection on the list is the financial architecture of Constellation Energy′s long−term sovereign tech contracts. The Crane Clean Energy Center (835MW) restart is backed by a 20−year Microsoft PPA and a heavily $1 billion DOE Loan Guarantee fixed at an ultra-low spread of just 37.5 basis points above U.S. Treasuries (maturing Oct 2055). At the same time, the Clinton 30 MW uprate is locked by a 20-year Meta PPA.

Apart from that, Constellation Energy's existing nuclear fleet is protected from downside commodity price risk through the Inflation Reduction Act's (and the 2025 OBBBA's) Nuclear Production Tax Credit [PTC] that is providing a transferable credit up to $15/MWh. As a result, it is protecting Constellation Energy's revenue until gross receipts hit the $26 to $44.75/MWh phase-out threshold. Overall, Constellation Energy holds the baseline needed for the 206 GW data center pipeline.

Eaton Corporation Stock (ETN)

For Eaton stock, the upside catalyst here is the 800V DC Transition and $9.5 billion Boyd Thermal Integration. As rack densities scale toward 1 megawatt to back NVIDIA's (NVDA) Rubin Ultra and Feynman architectures, traditional 480-volt alternating current [AC] distribution marks a physical copper and busbar limit. Eaton is actively shifting the industry towards 800-volt Direct Current [DC].

For more clarity, there is a 5% efficiency advantage. Current data center topologies have a ~5% electrical loss stepping down medium-voltage utility AC feeds to 54-volt rack DC. Eaton's integration of Resilient Power Systems ($86 million acquisition) supports medium-voltage solid-state transformers to convert utility feeds directly into DC. So, on every 100 GW national build-out, Eaton's architecture targets 5 GWs of stranded power from the grid.

More on this, Eaton is executing an aggressive horizontal integration strategy. By acquiring Boyd Thermal for $9.5B (closing Q2-2026), Eaton is targeting the microchannel cold-plate architecture born from aerospace engineering. This drives Eaton's accessible TAM per data center megawatt from $2.9 million to $3.4 million. Eaton’s Electrical Americas Q4-FY2025 orders grew 16% on a 12-month rolling basis. Along with that, data center orders up ~200% Y/Y and Backlog expanded by 31% Y/Y to $13.2 billion with a solid margin profile for 2026.

Vertiv Holdings Stock (VRT)

The bullish catalyst for Vertiv stock is the 45°C Fluid Dynamics Reality and the $1.138 billion PurgeRite acquisition. The recent market panic regarding NVIDIA’s CES comments that indicates next-gen chips can run on warmer water (45°C) and thus eliminate chillers, is a misinterpretation of thermodynamics. My point is that warmer water allows for more free cooling, but rejecting heat 24/7/365 in non-Arctic climates needs mechanical assistance during peak ambient temperatures. VRT has solved this with the Trim Cooler. This is a hybrid large dry cooler paired with a smaller mechanical chiller.

Focusing on the moat, pumping ultra-clean, chemically stable liquid through 100-MW campuses is beyond an IT task. In my opinion, it is heavy-industrial fluid management. Vertiv’s $1.138 billion acquisition of PurgeRite gives them the single-seller state on secondary fluid network commissioning, flushing, and lifecycle maintenance. On this, Vertiv’s Q4-FY2025 financial trends are important to back my argument. Organic orders accelerated ~252% Y/Y, and that is pushing the book-to-bill ratio to 2.9x. The backlog more than doubled to $15 billion (+109% Y/Y). Vertiv brought-in $910 million in Q4 adjusted FCF (+151% YoY) with an operating margin of 23.2%. The catalysts make Vertiv a tollbooth for preventing thermal shutdown at the rack level.

The Bear Case For The Picks For AI-Energy Alpha

Eaton is deploying $1.5 billion into 24 capital projects to scale up Electrical Americas capacity. Adding manufacturing headcount and depreciation ahead of the sales ramp forms friction. Eaton may have a 130 basis point margin headwind in 2026, front-loaded in Q1/Q2. More so, Eaton is executing the spin-off of the $3 billion Mobility unit by Q1-2027, and this is a big operational distraction, in my opinion. On the positive, Eaton's structural pricing power overrides this. It maintains a 30% margin in Electrical Americas despite these ramp costs, and can have $13–$13.50 Adjusted EPS for FY2026.

On Vertiv's side, it may be forced to increase CapEx to 3%-4% of sales in 2026 (up from historical 2-3%) to fund brownfield expansions in the Americas and greenfield sites in Asia. If the 12-to-18 month backlog delivery timeline faces construction delays from the client side, Vertiv's working capital cash inflows (that popped to $1.004 billion operating cash flow in Q4, largely based on advanced milestone payments) can reverse into a liquidity drain, in my stance.

Lastly, Constellation Energy has a massive $12.9 billion Nuclear Decommissioning Asset Retirement Obligation [ARO]. This ARO is highly sensitive to discount rates. I mean, a 50 bps decrease in CARFR increases the ARO by $125 million. More so, to satisfy the DOJ settlement for the Calpine merger, Constellation Energy must divest 6 power plants by September 4, 2026. A forced fire-sale in a tight regulatory window can lead to an unfavorable recovery of capital.

Conclusion

All in all, the AGI infrastructure shift is beyond a software play as it is an aggressive mobilization of electrical physics and thermodynamics. CEG stock is the anchor with the 55 GW baseload needed to power the 206 GW pipeline backed by federal PTC floors and 20-year hyperscaler PPAs. Whereas, Eaton stock has the efficiency arbitrage with the 800V DC power routing and the $3.4 million/MW wallet share expansion through the $9.5 billion Boyd Thermal integration. Vertiv stock is linked with hyper-growth based on a $15 billion backlog, 252% order growth, and the inescapable fluid dynamics (PurgeRite and the Trim Cooler). I see that Wall Street is playing musical chairs with trillion-dollar silicon valuations, but kind of ignoring the caps of the physical grid. In my opinion, to own the silicon stock is to speculate on the algorithm, but to own CEG, ETN, and VRT stocks is to own the thermodynamics basing the algorithm itself.

data.title: February Jobs Report: Signs Of Slowdown, But Rate Cut Unlikely
data.description:
Recent geopolitical stresses have raised the risks of a slowdown, as an extended oil price shock can't be ruled out. The latest labor market report, only supports the possibility.

In a jobs market that hasn't even been impacted by the US-Iran war yet, the latest developments aren't positive. In fact, they suggest that the first signs of some slowing could well be here, which in turn can be exacerbated by the ongoing tensions. Here, I look at what the report says, followed by what it means for monetary policy in the context of the larger situation and the best investing options.

What the labor market report says

In February 2026, unemployment rate inched up to 4.4%, taking it back to December 2025 levels, after dropping to 4.3% in January. This number isn't a matter of real concern, though, considering that the Fed targets exactly this rate for 2026.

The real story in the latest jobs report is the changes to non-farm payrolls, which were a shocker. The figure dropped by 92k, a sharp turnaround from a 130k increase in January 2026, as per the initial estimate. While there's some comfort to be gained from the fact that the drop is partly explained by strikes in the healthcare sector, it's not enough. Only losses of 28k were seen on this count, so 70% of the drop in payrolls is still unexplained.

A closer look at the data across industries reveals early signs of a slowing down in the economy. These are supported by revisions to past data and bigger picture trends over time. The details are as follows:

  • Cyclical cooling off: Industries like manufacturing and construction, as well as leisure and hospitality, have reported job losses of over 10k each. Since these are cyclical sectors, the latest numbers could well indicate a coming slowdown.
  • Downward revisions: Payroll figures for December 2025 have been revised downwards quite dramatically. As against the initial estimate of 48k job gains, 17k jobs are now estimated to have been lost during the month. For January 2026, the revisions were better, but the additions were still reduced by 4k to 126k.
  • Average additions see sharp drop: The bigger picture shows job losses in five of the last 12 months (see chart below). The government shutdown in October 2025 makes the numbers look worse, but that's just one month. In any case, even excluding it, the average additions to non-farm payrolls over the past 12 months is ~27k. By comparison, for the 12 months ending February 2025, the average additions were at 89.3k.

Rate cut probability drops

Just going by the labor market data, it would appear peculiar that the probability of a rate cut has reduced since I last checked following the CPI print for January 2026. At the time, only a 9.2% likelihood of a rate reduction existed already. The number is now down to just 3.7%, as the chart below shows.

But then this probability needs to be considered holistically. Escalating stresses between the US and Iran have been a key development in recent days. The macroeconomic impact is already becoming obvious, as, among other things, it has led to dizzying increases in crude oil prices.

WTI crude is up by 38% since the war began at the end of February. Crude price is at USD 91/bbl right now, the highest since July 2022, when it had risen due to Russia's invasion of Ukraine and supply chain challenges following COVID-19.

If the situation continues, an increase in inflation is on the cards, which in turn can affect growth. But keeping inflation in check will be, as has been the case historically, the Fed's top priority. And that, in turn, reduces the likelihood of a rate cut.

Stock market response

The stock markets were understandably unhappy with the labor market report, with the three key indices all losing value in yesterday's session. The NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) (QQQ) saw the biggest drop of 1.5%, followed by the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY), which was down by 1.3%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) dropped by around 1%.

Where to invest

Sectors like energy and defense have been big gainers this year, which is a no-brainer. A 27% rise in the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) YTD has been witnessed, while the Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA) is up by 15%.

They can bring further short-term gains, but that raises the ethical challenge of profiting from war. Instead, considering defensives like consumer staples and utilities might be a good idea now. Based on the latest labor market report and risks to inflation, they too can offer short-term gains.

Among the two defensive sectors, utilities, as seen from the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU), looks better placed. It's rated "Buy" by Seeking Alpha analysts, although Quant Rating still gives it a "Hold." But it's still better off than the State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Inc. ETF (XLP), which gets a Hold across both rating types. To me, this suggests there could be single stocks, or XLU peers that have more convincing ratings and can make better buys.

What's next?

The latest labor market report is yet another reminder of how fast investing scenarios can evolve. Just last month, I made a case for cyclicals. Now, there's one for defensives. In the relatively short term, of course.

What has remained unchanged, however, is the market view that no rate cuts will happen when the Fed meets later this month. Even if the jobs report would have made a case for easier monetary policy, the risk to inflation just rose too significantly to ignore.

If geopolitical stresses subside soon enough, though, the focus could go back to growth, which doesn't look good as per the latest labor market report. This too can change, but that remains to be seen.

data.title: Camp4 Therapeutics: Strong Cash Runway As CMP-002 Moves Toward Human Trials
data.description:
Thesis

Camp4 Therapeutics Corporation (CAMP) has just reported an FY25 GAAP EPS of -$2.65, a figure that misses analyst expectations by about $0.77. We’re seeing continued losses typical of where they are as a clinical-stage biotech with a hefty R&D spend. Revenue, on the other hand, was in line with estimates at $3.5 million, which was about a 438.5% year-over-year increase, but still only minimal and was just some collaboration-related income. The big bonus here was that they managed to end the year with $109.5 million in cash, up from $64.0 million back in 2024. Management gave a decent runway expectation, and with the upcoming milestones in 2026, this is going to be important. Now, Camp4 has a lot of early promise in its lead candidate, CMP-002, and I think we could see upside for the stock based on clinical milestones for this candidate over the next couple of years, as I'll explain.

Camp4 Therapeutics FY25/4Q25 results

If we take a look at the financials, Camp4 hinted at several strategic developments going on that boosted their liquidity even though they’re going through a pretty capital-intensive period. They managed to end FY25 with about $109.5 million in cash and cash equivalents, a significant jump from the $64.0 million we saw back in 2024. Now, this was mainly due to their financing activities; we saw an oversubscribed private placement providing about $50 million in upfront funding with the potential for another $50 million later on. There was also a $30 million underwritten equity offering, as well as that $17.5 million collaboration payment from GlaxoSmithKline (GSK). As a result, the balance sheet is looking far stronger, and management has tipped for a cash runway extending sometime into 2028.

Operationally speaking, research and development expenses stayed pretty flat at $38.2 million compared with $38.8 million in 2024. So there is continued investment in pipeline programs going on, particularly CMP-002 targeting SYNGAP1-related disorders; however, there were some reductions, especially with CMP-001 and a lease modification gain, which slightly offset costs. Elsewhere, general and administrative expenses increased to $17.4 million, up from $14.9 million last year, which I think we can put down to higher professional/corporate support costs we usually see with small biotechs scaling operations and managing collaborations.

Overall, they reported a net loss of about $80.4 million, a lot more compared to the $51.8 million loss in 2024. But keep in mind, this bigger loss can, for the most part, be put down to a $29.8 million non-cash accounting loss. This was related to the fair-value adjustment of derivative tranche liabilities from the September financing. Revenue was pretty minimal at just $3.5 million, which was really just some early collaboration revenue. So I think it’s safe to say that Camp4 is showing signs of transitioning toward clinical development here. Liquidity is up significantly, and research costs are being kept in line. So overall, the company is in a decent position to initiate that global Phase 1/2 clinical trial for CMP-002 as early as the second 2H26, while still being able to explore partnership opportunities for CMP-001.

Current pipeline

Just as a quick overview of the pipeline, Camp4 is pretty focused on regulatory RNA-targeting antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) therapeutics. These would be designed to increase gene expression and thus restore deficient protein levels in some genetic diseases. As you know, their lead candidate, CMP-002, targets the SYNGAP1 gene for the treatment of SYNGAP1-related neurodevelopmental disorders. Currently, it’s in late preclinical development with GLP toxicology studies underway, but the company has plans to start a first-in-human Phase 1/2 clinical trial as early as the 2H26. Elsewhere, they have also developed CMP-001 for urea cycle disorders. Again, focusing on increasing protein levels, this works by upregulating CPS1 expression to help restore urea cycle function. This program actually entered Phase 1 clinical testing; however, they’ve since paused further internal investment and are instead exploring partnership opportunities to continue development. Now, beyond just these two programs, CAMP4 is advancing some very early discovery-stage candidates in the central nervous system and metabolic diseases. This is where they have some collaboration, including one with GSK, which is for the discovery of additional RNA-based therapeutics targeting genes, specifically those involved in neurodegenerative and kidney diseases.

CMP-002 up to now

Since CMP-002 would be the big near-term catalyst for Camp4, it's worth taking a deep look at its development. It’s a first-in-class ASO therapy tipped to target the underlying genetic cause of SYNGAP1-related neurodevelopmental disorder. This is a pretty severe condition in which we see intellectual disability, epilepsy, and developmental delay. Mechanistically speaking, the CMP-002 job here is really quite simple on paper. It works by targeting regulatory RNAs that control the transcription of the SYNGAP1 gene. So by using Camp4’s RAP platform to identify the specific regulatory elements governing gene expression, the ASO can then bind to these regulatory RNAs and amplify the transcription of the SYNGAP1 gene. This should then increase messenger RNA production with the ultimate end goal of raising SYNGAP protein levels in neurons. And because SYNGAP1 disease is caused by haploinsufficiency, in which we only see one functional copy of the gene, leading to about a 50% reduction in protein levels, even a very modest increase in expression levels here could meaningfully improve synaptic function and thus neurological outcomes. As a therapy, the current design is an injection into the cerebrospinal fluid to reach the central nervous system directly.

So far, the preclinical results supporting CMP-002 have been rather encouraging. In humanised SYNGAP1 haploinsufficient mouse models, we’ve seen that a single dose restored SYNGAP protein levels close to normal and also produced a dose-dependent increase in protein expression across brain regions. Even some experiments showed up to about a 1.7-fold increase in protein levels. What’s more impressive is that the treated animals also showed some improvements in disease-relevant behavioural phenotypes. There was better motor performance, reduced hyperactivity, and some improvements in learning/memory tasks. On top of this, treatment of non-human primate studies also demonstrated that repeated intrathecal dosing was pretty well tolerated and produced significant increases in the SYNGAP protein. Again, this was across multiple brain regions, which would suggest adequate CNS distribution and pharmacodynamic activity. This latter part here is a pretty important translational signal for human trials.

Global Phase 1/2 clinical trial for CMP-002

So right now, Camp4 is conducting some GLP toxicology studies required for regulatory approval. They aim to start clinical testing soon with plans to launch a global first-in-human Phase 1/2 trial as early as 2H26. I think we will see the trial follow a typical rare-disease ASO study design. So first we will have dose-escalation cohorts to evaluate safety/tolerability, and pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics after intrathecal administration. The next step would be expansion cohorts to assess preliminary efficacy in patients. At this stage, the key endpoints I’ll be looking out for will be safety and tolerability, particularly with respect to CNS adverse events, which can be a major roadblock for some gene therapy programs.

Of course, we want to see biomarker evidence of increased SYNGAP protein expression or downstream signaling changes. Eventually, early clinical signals like improvements in seizure frequency, cognitive function, or neurodevelopmental measures would potentially be a massive outcome for Camp4. Now, since SYNGAP1 is a monogenic disorder with pretty clear mechanistic biomarkers, even if we see just modest increases in protein expression or improvements in EEG or behavioural endpoints, this could actually be considered proof of concept. As for a timeframe, we are still very early in development, but I think if the study begins in late 2026, early safety and biomarker readouts could realistically emerge sometime in 2027. Keep this timeframe in mind before investing, as Camp4 has very little in terms of upcoming clinical catalysts, and we may see stock volatility based on earnings, before shares build good momentum on the back of clinical readouts. The more meaningful clinical efficacy signals would potentially appear much later, with 2028 as a rough timeframe, but this depends heavily on enrollment and follow-up duration. This would also represent the first human validation of Camp4’s regulatory RNA-targeting platform, so it's quite a big step forward in the right direction.

CAMP stock valuation

As for a fair valuation of Camp4 stock, it currently trades at a market cap of about $238 million with an enterprise value of just $169 million, because of the company’s decent cash balance relative to just minimal debt. And since Camp4 is still a clinical-stage biotech without any real commercial products, traditional profitability metrics aren't that meaningful, so we would be better served to use revenue/balance-sheet multiples here. In that sense, the EV to sales multiple sits at 44.5x and the price to sales at 25.6x. Now, both are far above the healthcare sector medians of roughly 3x to 4x sales, but keep in mind the company only generated about $3.5 million in revenue. So these multiples would appear expensive on a purely financial basis, but it’s pretty typical for where the company is at.

The market is mainly pricing Camp4 on its pipeline potential. They hold a price-to-book ratio of 3.7x, which is somewhat closer to sector norms, but also reflective of that strengthened balance sheet after the recent financing and collaboration payments. Overall, the strategic progress of Camp4’s RNA-targeting platform and the advancement of CMP-002 this year would make up the valuation potential here. There should also be some upside from its collaboration with GSK, which investors are pricing in.

Looking ahead

Looking ahead, the long-term value in Camp4 stock here is to build momentum on the back of CMP-002. It’s both a rare disease, and we typically see a high value assigned to disease-modifying genetic therapies. As for a brief TAM estimate, SYNGAP1-related disorder is estimated to occur in about 6.1 to 10 people per 100,000 individuals; however, far fewer people have actually been formally identified worldwide, with Camp4’s estimate being just over 10,000 patients in the U.S. So it would indicate substantial underdiagnosis, and a potentially larger addressable population could exist here with genetic testing expansion. This, of course, places CMP-002 in the category of ultra-rare neurological disorders, in which we usually see very small patient populations supporting meaningful commercial opportunity. We should also note here that there are currently no approved disease-modifying therapies for SYNGAP1 on the market. The current treatment only consists of supportive interventions such as behavioural therapy and seizure management. So it means the competitive landscape for Camp4 here is relatively open, since most efforts are still in early research or preclinical development.

From a commercial viewpoint, investors usually benchmark rare genetic therapies against other one-time or disease-modifying gene/RNA therapies. We usually see these command hefty prices between roughly $850,000 and $3 million per patient, depending on durability and clinical benefit. So early-stage trials for gene therapy can take on quite a bit of stock momentum as a result. If CMP-002 does ultimately demonstrate meaningful increases in SYNGAP1 protein expression, investors could begin modeling a rare-disease therapy with pricing in the high six-figure to multi-million-dollar range, but keep in mind it is still very early on. So, to recap, the key catalysts over the next few years will be initiation of the Phase 1/2 trial and enrollment. Then, early biomarker confirmation of SYNGAP1 protein upregulation. As the company is in a pretty good position cash wise to support the trial, with a lot of the equity raising already done, I do see more upside for this stock over the next couple of years.

data.title: ARK Innovation ETF: The Trading Strategy Is Now Flashing 'Sell' (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My most recent article on the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), titled "Time To Go All In" and published in October 2025, deserves a meme thumbnail (see below). As it turns out, the timing of my bullish report almost perfectly matched the stock's peak price level achieved since the beginning of the inflation and interest rate shock of 2022, at $93 per share. From there, the ETF declined by about 18% over less than five months, while the S&P 500 (SPY) remained largely flat.

While I deserve to (and will gladly) poke fun at the poor timing of my article, I think that the trading strategy that I discussed last year deserves a closer inspection. Rather than considering it a failure because of the double-digit losses produced recently, I think that the "sell" signal that it is now flashing should be taken seriously by those who buy into the idea of holding the fund for long-term price appreciation while cutting the downside to prevent sizable losses.

On the Fundamentals of ARKK

Before diving into the trading strategy, let me address the fundamentals of the ARK Innovation ETF.

The fund, largely speaking, seeks long-term gains from exposure to the technologies of the future: electric and autonomous vehicles, represented by ARKK's still largest holding, Tesla (TSLA), at 10.3% allocation; digital currency, expressed through the likes of Coinbase (COIN) at 4.8% allocation, which is a bit lower than the 6% as of early October; gaming and media, among others (see allocation chart below). The ETF is large at an AUM of $6.7 billion, liquid at an average daily trading volume of 8.5 million shares, and charges a hefty management fee of 75 bps.

My views on ARKK's investment philosophy have not changed in the past half-year. I still believe that, strategically, the approach makes sense and has a decent chance of outperforming, over the next couple of decades, bets in "old economy" sectors like oil and gas, industrials, etc.

But tactically, I continue to have the same reservations as before. First, the ARKK "quasi-black box" model allows for quite a bit of portfolio management discretion when choosing what to invest in and how much. Worse, the team's track record is iffy at best. The ETF has massively underperformed the Nasdaq 100 index (QQQ) since inception, other than during the "buy everything" melt-up of the earlier COVID-19 days that was driven by extra-loose monetary and fiscal policies—see the chart below.

Second, I also dislike Cathy Wood's trademark idea of trimming stock positions when valuations rise and accumulating shares when valuations dip, which is another way of saying, "Keep the losers, ditch the winners". This approach is consistent with either value investing or short-term tactical allocation that aims at benefiting from cyclical swings in prices. However, it is inconsistent with long-term investing in secular growth stories, in my view.

On the Trading Strategy

A quick look at the ARKK price chart (above) will probably make it clear that, at least historically, the ETF has displayed trending behavior and high volatility. I believe that the latter is disruptive and pretty much always detrimental to a portfolio's risk-adjusted performance.

However, the former can be an asset for investors who are diligent at riding the upside of a robust growth story while trimming losses before they become catastrophic. This is what is at the core of my ARKK trading strategy of owning shares while the 50-day moving average is above the 200-day moving average and then selling shares when the opposite is true.

Following this approach wouldn't have prevented an investor from incurring the 18% losses of the past five months. But for a very volatile ETF like ARKK (36% annualized volatility since the 2014 inception, which suggests twice the risk of investing in the Nasdaq 100), such a dip should be considered "business as usual". Investors who disagree should probably stay away from ARKK altogether.

The chart below shows that the two moving averages are now crossing for the first time since June 2025, with the 50-day line heading lower this time (worth noting, between the buy signal of June 2025 and today's sell signal, ARKK climbed 14% from $65 to $74, precisely matching the absolute returns of the S&P 500). This is the sell signal that might prevent investors who still believe in the long-term potential of ARKK from riding the roller coaster further down.

Lastly, I should point out that going all in or all out on ARKK does not mean betting a substantial portion of one's portfolio on this ETF, regardless of loss-mitigation tactics that might be deployed. Since the end of 2019, for example, ARKK has produced an annualized volatility of 47% and a maximum drawdown of 81%, while the trading strategy would have trimmed both to 33% and 48%, respectively. Still, despite the stop-loss triggers, these risk metrics are quite high for most investors, so diversification is highly advisable.

While individual risk tolerance and the characteristics of a portfolio's other holdings should dictate how much one might want to allocate to ARKK, I think that anything above 10% is speculative. Regardless, today, my trading strategy says that exposure to this ETF should head to zero and stay there until ARKK finds its footing once again.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Greg Abel Is Killing Two Birds With One Stone (Upgrade)
data.description:
If you look at my coverage history of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A), you will notice that all my ratings were a hold, and you will also see that in all of them the stock was very close to $500. In addition, you will see that in all of them Warren Buffett was in the picture of the article, but this time I decided to put Greg Abel for obvious reasons.

And now BRKB stock is at $498, a very small difference from my last article, so in theory, the valuation hasn't changed much, and since the long-term case hasn't changed either, it would make sense for me to maintain a more cautious sentiment, right?

Well, more or less. My second article was about three reasons I'm not buying Berkshire stock, one of which is already being addressed by Greg Abel, which is the use of cash, but I believe that with this he is also addressing another concern I had, which was the lack of buybacks, which showed how the company itself did not think the stock was in a “buy” moment.

The 2 Problems Greg Abel Is Solving:

Well, the first point here (the huge cash pile) ends up mixing several factors. But they all revolve around Berkshire Hathaway's cash and equivalents. I wasn't so comfortable, and I'm still not 100% comfortable with such a large cash position. This may seem contradictory, especially since I just wrote an article about how now seems like a good time to have dry powder in short-term Treasuries (check it out here). It's still an absurd amount of cash, but it has decreased somewhat in the last quarter.

Personally, having short-term Treasuries not only helps the investor to ride out these more volatile moments, but it also allows me to buy stocks I like when they fall, as was the case with Amazon (AMZN) recently, and could even be the case with BRK.B eventually. But the problem is that this does not exactly apply to Berkshire. Here, I am talking about an individual's cash on hand, and with my current net worth, I can buy any stock in the world without any liquidity issues, which is quite different from Berkshire. Just to give an example, a few months ago I increased my position in Inter (INTR) a little. For Berkshire to achieve a minimally relevant position in Inter, it would take them years, or rather, they would have to buy the entire company and take it private, and even then, they would only spend ~$3.5 billion of their cash on hand.

$3.5 billion is literally less than 1% of current cash. And that's why I constantly asked myself, what are they going to buy now? Mag-7? Are they going to make a big acquisition of a private company or buy several stakes in small companies? Well, I don't know, but that's the job of Berkshire Hathaway's managers, to figure out what they're going to buy and how it will increase shareholder value. And even if it takes time, I hope they can find some direction for the cash that is truly capable of creating value and not just something like “index-like returns,” which is what would happen if they only had stakes in large companies.

This problem is being addressed in part because Berkshire Hathaway has just announced that it will resume its buyback program. Of course, nothing is guaranteed, and we don't know if this will be enough to reduce, I don't know, even 10% of the cash (it seems to me that in 1-2 years it could happen), but this is interesting because this buyback is precisely in the “Buffett style”; there is no commitment to a minimum or maximum amount, minimum or maximum time. Basically, as long as/when BRK shares are at a valuation that management believes will deliver an attractive internal rate of return (IRR), Berkshire can buy. It seems like a great position to me.

Berkshire’s Price: Now It’s Better

Continuing on the subject of buybacks, this is not only a way of addressing the big cash pile “problem,” it is also an endorsement (and a huge endorsement at that) that the valuation is attractive again. It may seem that little has changed, as I mentioned, the stock is still practically at $500, but note how the P/B has already fallen from 1.6x to below 1.5x.

For me, this is already a sign that Berkshire is no longer so overvalued. Even though the BRK.B price hasn't changed in about a year, the company has changed. BNSF has grown its results of operations by 9% since 2024, and Berkshire Hathaway Energy (BHE) has grown by 7%, so Berkshire is improving (even though investment gains are hindering it due to the stronger comparative base), while the price stays the same, i.e., the margin of safety is widening.

Other than that, we are talking about a company with a lot of real assets, a huge cash pile of over $300B, a railroad business that is easily worth tens of billions, a strong insurance business, dozens of billions in Apple (AAPL) stocks alone and tens more in American Express (AXP), and its market cap is still at $1T, meaning it is not as if it were massively overvalued. And that makes it more appealing to me, because management is making moves that I like, such as significantly reducing the position in Apple and prioritizing stocks with a more attractive valuation.

Has Greg Abel Proven His Worth? Kinda

An important point here is that believing that the return from Berkshire's decisions will be good going forward depends on our conviction in Berkshire's management. It is a management business, a people business - actually, in the end, almost all businesses are, but this one is even more dependent on the good decisions of the managers.

Particularly, I think Greg Abel has already proven his value, at least partially; the management of assets like BHE has been very good in recent years, and this signal that he purchased about $15 million and will continue buying BRK.B stocks with his salary is, for me, a sign of gigantic cultural green flag.

I still need to mention that this is only “kinda”, because the bar is really high. Greg Abel is replacing the best investor of all time.

When I went to the Berkshire Hathaway annual meeting in Omaha last year, it became clear to me that Buffett ended up becoming a symbol, isn't a regular earnings/results call anymore, it’s something that goes way beyond that, almost like a mystique. And what I mean by this is that, as much as the Berkshire stock wasn't doing well, the quality of management in Buffett's hands was indisputable; hedge funds weren't going to sell Berkshire stock just because the stock wasn't rising as much as the Nasdaq, and neither were the retail investors.

Now I wonder, if under Greg Abel's management Berkshire continues sideways for 2-3 years, will investors have the necessary patience to wait for him to make a decision calmly, and for it to be a good move? Or will they pressure him with news in media portals, selling the stock and all that? It's hard to say, because Greg Abel still needs to prove himself in this part, taking this Berkshire cash and making some acquisition of a few dozen billions that is truly interesting and synergistic, as it was with BNSF or Geico. It seems to me that he has the potential, and this requires not only vision, it also needs some opportunity to appear, and the market will need to be patient and give the benefit of the doubt.

This is also one of the main risks of the Berkshire stock, losing this quality premium that it has sustained for a long time, whether due to some bad decisions or even a lack of decisions in the coming years. Of course, the operational risk of BNSF, insurance, and BHE also exist, but the solidity is so great that I think the real risk lies in the asset management part - increasing a position in a stock that won't give as much return as AAPL, spending all this cash on something that won't give as much return, or even leaving this cash in Treasury for years and years, missing opportunities in the market; and although I don't think this will happen, we will need to monitor the next quarters to see.

With this endorsement, and a Berkshire position better than in recent quarters regarding valuation, I am upgrading my rating to buy, but still in a cautious way, since the asymmetry in the stock is still not large; it is just allowing us to buy BRK.B stock with a margin of safety that is “fair enough”, and is not yet “great”.

data.title: 5 Relatively Secure And Cheap Dividend Stocks, Yields Up To 8% (March 2026)
data.description:
************

Author's Note: This is our monthly series on Dividend Stocks, usually published in the first week of every month. We scan the universe of roughly 7,500 stocks listed and traded on U.S. exchanges and use our proprietary filtering criteria to select five relatively safe stocks that may be trading cheaper compared to their historical valuations. Some of the sections in the article, like "Selection Process/Methodology," are repeated each month with few changes. This is intentional as well as unavoidable, as this is necessary for the new readers to be able to conceptualize the process. Regular readers of this series could skip such sections to avoid repetitiveness and jump to the section titled "Selection of the Top 50." Readers can also read a more detailed explanation of the goals and the selection process in our blog post here.

************

Introduction and Market Summary:

Since Nov. 2025, the market has been mostly trading in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 6950. Though the S&P 500 did cross the SPX 7000 level briefly in January, it has not done so in any meaningful way. There have been so many developments in the last month or so, ranging from slightly cooling inflation (with some mixed signals), the Fed holding interest rates steady, the tariff situation in light of the Supreme Court order, and, more recently, the fast-evolving geopolitical situation around Iran. On top of all that, the AI overvaluations and overborrowing have gripped the markets for some time. Also, the fear of the AI's impact on software stocks has resulted in brutal revaluations on almost every company in the segment, though it may be overdone. The trend from 2025, the outperformance of the foreign markets (over the U.S. market), is still alive and ongoing. Also, there appears to be a rotation of big money from technology to industrial, materials, energy, and utility sectors that is continuing. In the short term, the market is likely to have elevated volatility due to the ongoing developments in the Iran war situation. Obviously, the oil prices are on the rise, and so are the gold prices.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatch Tool website.

All that being said, does it impact how we should invest? Probably not. We should invest for the long term, with our time-tested and proven strategies on a continual basis, and buy more when opportunities exist. Nonetheless, most times, it is hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

It is always good to have some idea of where the markets and the broader economy may be headed. Even so, we believe markets are always unpredictable, and it is not possible to catch the exact bottom (or the peak). So, it is best to invest regularly and consistently in good, solid dividend-paying stocks when their valuations are reasonable or attractive.

This month, we highlight three groups with five stocks each that have an average dividend yield (as a group) of 3.88%, 6.68%, and 7.50%, respectively. The first list is for conservative and risk-averse investors, while the second is for investors seeking higher yields but still wanting relatively safe dividends. The third group is for yield-hungry investors but comes with an elevated risk, and we urge investors to exercise caution.

The Selection Process:

Goals and Objectives

The main goal of this series of articles is to shortlist and highlight companies that have a solid history of paying and raising dividends. In addition, we demand that these companies support strong fundamentals, carry low debt, and be offered at a relatively cheaper valuation. These DGI stocks are not going to make anyone rich overnight, but if your goal is to attain financial freedom by owning stocks that will grow dividends over time, meaningfully and sustainably, then you are at the right place. These lists are not necessarily recommendations to buy but a shortlist of probable candidates for further research. Besides, we think each month, if nothing else, this analysis is able to highlight some nuggets that would have otherwise skipped our attention.

Every month, we start with roughly 7,500 stocks that are listed and traded on U.S. exchanges, including over-the-counter (OTC) networks. Using our filtering criteria, the initial list is quickly narrowed to less than 600 stocks, mostly dividend-paying and dividend-growing. From then on, by using various data elements, including dividend history, payout ratios, revenue growth, debt ratios, EPS growth, etc., we calculate a "Dividend Quality Score" for each stock that measures the relative safety and sustainability of the dividend. In addition to dividend safety, we also seek cheaper valuations. We also demand that the selected companies have an established business model, solid dividend history, manageable debt, and investment-grade credit rating.

Note: All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, Morningstar, and Investors.com.

Criteria to Shortlist:

  • Market cap > $7 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high-quality but lower-yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0.

By applying the above criteria, we get between 500 and 600 companies.

Narrowing Down the List:

As a first step, we would like to eliminate stocks that have less than five years of dividend history. Also, since we want a lot of flexibility and a wider choice at this initial stage, we include some companies that pay dividends lower than the S&P 500 but otherwise have a stellar dividend record and growing dividends at a fast pace.

However, so far in this list, we have demanded five or more years of consistent dividend growth. But what if a company had a very stable record of dividend payments but did not increase the dividends from one year to another? At times, some of these companies are foreign-based companies, and due to currency fluctuations, their dividends may appear to have been cut in U.S. dollars, but in reality, that may not be true at all when looked at in the actual currency of reporting. At times, we may provide some exceptions when a company may have cut the dividend in the past, but otherwise, it looks compelling.

We now import the various data elements from many sources and assign weights based on different criteria as listed below:

  • Current yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio based on EPS
  • Past three-year and five-year dividend growth
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next five years' growth)
  • Chowder number- This number has been named after the well-known SA author Chowder, who first coined and popularized this factor. This number is derived by adding the current yield and the past five years' dividend growth rate. A Chowder number of "12" or more ("8" for utilities) is considered good.
  • Debt/Capital ratio
  • Debt/Asset ratio
  • S&P's credit rating
  • Distance from 52-week high: This factor is calculated as (current price - 52-week high) / 52-week high.
  • 5-year sales or revenue growth.

Selection of the Top 50

Downloadable Dataset: Below, we provide a link to the table with relevant data on nearly 500 stocks. Readers can download this table for further analysis. Please note that the table is sorted by "Total Weight" or "Quality Score."

File-for-export_-_5_Secure_DGI_-_March_2026.xlsx

We will first bring down the list (of 400-plus names) to roughly 50 names by automated criteria, as listed below. In the second step, which is mostly manual and subjective, we will bring the list down to about 15.

  • Step 1: First, take the top 20 names from the above table (based on total weight or quality score). At times, some industry segments tend to get overcrowded at the top, so we take the top two (or three) and ignore the rest.
  • Step 2: As a second step, we will take the top 10 names based on the highest dividend yield. When it comes to dividend yield, some of the industry segments tend to be overcrowded. So, we will take the top two (or a maximum of three) names from any single industry segment.
  • Step 3: Now, we will sort our list based on five-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 4: We will now consider more recent dividend growth, as that is more indicative of the company's future growth. We will sort our list based on 3-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 5: We also want to give priority to stocks that are rated highest in terms of credit rating. So, we will sort the list based on the numerical weight of the credit rating and select the top 10 stocks with the best credit rating. Again, we are careful not to have too many names from the same sector.
  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ADP, AU, BRO, CI, MSFT, VRT, and WIT (7 duplicates).

Stocks that appeared three times: HWM (2 duplicates).

After removing eight duplicates, we are left with 61 (70-9) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than <= 2% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 3-year) was less than 10%, we would rather remove them at this stage.

So, we remove WMT, AMP, and CME. After removing, we are left with 58 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment. The criteria are to keep the names that have reasonable dividend yields, dividend growth, and valuation.

On this basis, we remove a total of 18 names:

PAYX, INTU, ORCL, ACN, BSY, MPWR, COKE, BBVA, TD, EWBC, AXP, AEG, PGR, TS, LLY, NVO, IMO, and WES.

After we remove these 18 names, we are left with 40 (58-18) names.

The top names from each sector/industry segment are presented:

Financial Services, Banking, and Insurance:

Banks- Regional:

Banks- Major & Regional: (NWG)

Bank (Foreign): (CIB)

Financial Services - Others: (OWL), (BX), (ARCC), (AGNC)

Security & Exchanges –

Insurance: (BRO), (ORI)

Business Services/Consulting:

(V), (ADP)

Conglomerates:

Industrials:

(PH), (NDSN), (VRT), (GE)

Construction & Engg: (HWM), (FIX)

Transportation/Logistics:

Materials/Chemicals/Mining/Gold:

Chemicals:

Materials - others:

Mining (other than Gold):

Gold: (AU)

Defense/Aerospace:

Consumer/Retail/Others:

Cons-Staples: (PG), (CAG)

Cons-discretionary: (TJX), (RL), (TKO)

Tobacco: (MO)

Communications/Media

(TU)

Healthcare:

Pharma: (MRK), (PFE)

Healthcare Insurance: (CI), (UNH)

Healthcare Services:

Technology:

Software & Services: (MSFT), (WIT)

Hardware: (HPQ)

Semiconductor & Electronics: (APH), (AVGO)

Energy:

Energy Majors: (CVX)

Oil & Gas (prod. & exploration): (SLB), (CVE)

Pipelines/Midstream: (MPLX)

Refining:

Utilities:

(SBS)

Housing/Construction:

Home Building:

Building Material:

REIT:

(GLPI), (ARE).

Final Step: Narrowing Down to Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40, we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with an "A" rating (from S&P)
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 3.88%

  • (MSFT)
  • (ADP)
  • (BX)
  • (CVX)
  • (PFE)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid, diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 3.88%, which is more than 3x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 33 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of "A-" or higher. If you must have higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

MSFT (Microsoft)

MSFT is currently trading at nearly 25% below its 52-week high. In fact, it has lost over 17% since the beginning of 2026. This year, the software stocks are having a terrible time due to AI fears. However, Microsoft is too diversified and entrenched in the enterprise processes. To think that it will be replaced by AI any time soon is too pessimistic a view. In fact, Microsoft, with its massive investments in OpenAI, is likely to benefit from the AI trends. Also, the company is one of only three large public cloud providers and can provide software solutions to enterprises at scale. The company's Azure business is still growing at nearly 30% annually. All that said, the stock price had gotten ahead of itself last year, and the current prices offer a great opportunity to own this company at lower prices. The yield is still very low at 0.93%, but we are including this company in the A-list for dividend growth and capital appreciation rather than yield.

ADP (Automatic Data Processing)

ADP's yield is moderate but has gone up considerably in recent times due to an ongoing price correction. The current dividend yield of 3.17% is very favorable compared to its 5-year average of 2%. It is often considered a favorite of dividend investors, given the company's stability and the reliability of the dividend and above-average dividend growth. It is relatively rare to find this company trading at cheaper valuations, except during deep market corrections. However, due to panic selling of software stocks due to perceived fear of AI takeover, it offers a unique opportunity. Investors have sold off their software stocks, irrespective of the quality. There is some argument that advancements in AI could result in improved capabilities of corporations to move payroll processing in-house, thereby impacting ADP's business. However, the fear is overdone and ignores the company's potential to use AI effectively to increase its business and reach.

Morningstar rates the stock price as undervalued by more than 25%, and the share price is nearly 35% below its 52-week high.

CVX (Chevron)

CVX frequently takes a spot on our A-list due to its relatively safe, moderately high dividend and excellent credit rating. Even though the energy sector is usually volatile, CVX is probably one of the best investments in its sector for stable income and reasonable returns over time. Chevron is known to be a low-cost producer with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of "AA" from S&P, which shows its financial strength.

However, recently, the energy sector has been gaining momentum, and especially due to the evolving geopolitical situation, oil prices and the company's stock price have gained considerably.

CVX currently offers an attractive dividend yield of 3.8%, but it is considerably lower than what it was last year. It is best to accumulate this stock over a period of time and take the benefit of dollar cost averaging.

PFE (Pfizer):

We are including Pfizer in all three lists this month. The reason is obviously the attractive and tempting yield of 6.2%. We always tend to include one company in the A-list with a slightly higher yield. However, we try to minimize the risks.

The health and pharma sector was highly undervalued in 2025 due to many ongoing uncertainties, but those uncertainties are gradually fading away. This has been reflected in the recovery of the sector in 2026. This has benefited Pfizer's stock price to some extent, and it has gained nearly 2525 from the lows of 2025. However, the prices and the yield are still attractive. In fact, the yield is still the highest amongst the major pharma companies, but the dividend growth is tepid. Further, Morningstar assigns it a 4-star rating, and the current price is nearly 20% below its estimate of fair value. The company enjoys an excellent credit rating, even though the acquisition of Seagen in 2023 and Metsera in 2025 added to Pfizer's debt.

Pfizer's pipeline has been improving with several successful recent drug launches and acquisitions. There will be some patent losses between 2026 and 2028, but the major ones will be towards the end of 2028. So, the company has ample time and potential to replace them with the new drugs.

BX (Blackstone Inc.):

Blackstone is one of the most respected alternative-asset managers. It has roughly $1.2 trillion in AUM (assets under management), out of which roughly over 72% generate fee-based revenue. Its major business/product lines include private equity, real estate (or real assets), private credit, and other alternatives. Blackstone has specialized in revitalizing property assets or companies by various means. Even though fundraising by alternative asset managers was challenging during 2022-2025 due to elevated interest rates, Blackstone was able to raise $150 billion or more in each of the past three years.

The company's shares have lost roughly 25% since the beginning of this year, mostly due to adverse industry-wide cyclical headwinds. That said, the company has faced such issues before, and due to its scale and management excellence, it has come out stronger each time. So, it may be a good time to open a position. In the meantime, the company generates a lot of fee-based revenue and pays over 80% of that in the form of dividends. The current dividend yield is 5.26%, but the dividend amount can vary greatly from year to year. The dividend growth has been patchy, but it has still grown 13% annualized in the last 5-6 years (since 2020). Morningstar assigns it a wide-moat rating in its industry and assigns a fair value nearly 35% above its current price.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% and 6.50%.
  • At least two names with an 'A-' (or above) rating.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 6.68%

  • (ADP)
  • (MPLX)
  • (GLPI)
  • (ARCC)
  • (PFE)

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield of 6.7%, an average of 26 years of dividend history, and 12 years (average) of consecutive growth.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

MPLX (MPLX LP)

MPLX LP makes it to our B-list and C-list on a regular basis. It is a highly attractive name from the midstream energy industry. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in the case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our B-list and C-list due to its high income, stable business, and decent dividend growth. There are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

ARCC (Ares Capital)

ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high- and low-interest-rate environments. The company offers a stable dividend. The current environment is somewhat challenging, to say the least, and that's why the valuation has come down significantly and offers a good opportunity for long-term investors.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers a highly attractive yield of roughly 10.3%. The most recent NII for the last quarter was $0.52 a share, which was still above the quarterly dividend of $0.48 a share, though the margin of coverage is much lower.

GLPI (Gaming and Leisure Properties):

GLPI is a real estate investment property trust, and it specializes in acquiring, financing, owning, and leasing real estate properties related to the gaming and leisure industry. GLPI leases its properties to gaming operators in triple-net lease arrangements.

GLPI came into existence as a REIT in 2013 due to the spinoff from Penn National Gaming. Since then, it has continually expanded its operations at a steady pace and now owns a portfolio of 69 gaming properties with over 15,000 hotel rooms spanning across 20 states. Most GLPI properties are in the northeastern and south-central parts of the United States. Its portfolio is considered high quality, with several tenants ranked number one in their respective markets. The only other publicly traded REIT in the gaming industry is VICI Properties (VICI), which is much larger than GLPI in terms of its assets and market cap.

GLPI's occupancy rates have been extremely good at 100% for most of its existence. It has a total of eight unique tenants; however, over 87% of the rents are received from just four tenants/operators. So, in that sense, it is rather tenant-concentrated, but it has had zero rent defaults so far. Also, this is just the nature of the gaming industry, as there are only a limited number of operators.

The company is currently paying a dividend of $0.78 per share, which increased from $0.76 per share in the year-ago period (and $0.73 two years ago). So, its dividend is $3.12 a year, and the AFFO in 2025 was $3.88, covering the dividend at 1.24x. The AFFO guidance for 2026 is between $4.06 and $4.11.

GLPI is a very stable cash-flow generator and a reliable dividend payer. With the exception of 2020, when it cut the dividends by about 10%, it has raised the dividends every year. It also pays a small amount in special dividends sometimes. The average dividend growth has been in the low single digits over the last 5 years and 10 years. The current yield is very attractive at 6.4% and slightly better than its five-year average of roughly 6%.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% and 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 7.50%

  • (MPLX)
  • (GLPI)
  • (ARCC)
  • (PFE)
  • (CAG)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

It is apparent from the selections that this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 7.50%. However, please note that this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but not nearly as safe as in the A-List or even the B-list. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk, as they are turnaround stories. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. Even then, it's a diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

CAG (Conagra Brands)

Conagra offers a very attractive dividend yield of 7.3%. At first, we should be alarmed by that level of dividend yield from a consumer staples company. So, apparently, there are risks that investors should be aware of before they make investments in this company. The company's stock price has been in a constant downtrend all of this year and has lost more than 50% since 2023. The stock has lost over 33% in 2025 alone, while the S&P 500 gained 19% in the same time period. However, that is in the past, and most of the downside appears to be priced in, and further downside may be limited, if any.

Now, some negatives and risks. The company does not enjoy any moat and is facing stiff competition and pricing pressures from private labels. Moreover, its balance sheet is not in a very strong situation, with net debt/adjusted-EBITDA of nearly 3.8x. Also, the dividend payout is somewhat elevated at 73%; however, the company is targeting to bring it down to 55% in the next few years. With that outlook, there is high income with potentially some risks. Only income investors with high-risk tolerance should consider this stock.

What if We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ADP, ARCC, GLPI, and MPLX (4 duplicates).

Three-time duplicates: PFE (2 duplicates).

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as nine different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 5.63%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -17.5%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 7%.

Average Payout Ratio: 72%.

Average Total Quality Score: 64.3.

Table 3:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 3.88%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.68%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with a 7.50% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 5.63% yield.

data.title: RadNet's Pullback Offers Upside With Outpatient Imaging And AI
data.description:
RadNet, Inc. (RDNT) is a healthcare company that provides outpatient imaging services. Its Digital Health segment, DeepHealth, offers workflow software and clinical AI for radiology operations. The company grows through joint ventures with hospitals, new centers, and acquisitions. Recently, RDNT acquired Gleamer, which is an AI-powered radiology company that will become part of its DeepHealth portfolio. And overall, I believe RDNT trades at compelling valuation multiples in light of its growth prospects (and secular AI trends), which is why I deem the stock a “Strong Buy” at these levels.

Promising Diagnostic Imaging Business

RadNet, Inc. is a healthcare company that provides diagnostic imaging services. It operates a large network of facilities, comprising 418 centers across the US, reporting the completion of more than 10 million outpatient imaging procedures annually. RDNT was founded back in 1981 and is currently headquartered in Los Angeles, California. However, they recently reported record revenues and completed a major AI acquisition, which I believe makes it a very interesting stock to cover.

You see, as a quick recap, RDNT’s Imaging Center Segment offers services such as MRI for soft tissue and CT for cross-sectional body images. Similarly, they provide PET scans that show activity in the body. RDNT’s centers also provide routine imaging like nuclear medicine for organ function, mammography, ultrasound, X-rays, fluoroscopy, and related procedures. Most centers offer several types of imaging in one location for convenience, so it’s a well-rounded business already in this niche.

Moreover, RDNT also has a Digital Health segment that develops and sells software and clinical applications to support imaging operations and interpretation. This includes eRAD, which offers software like radiology information systems (RIS) and picture archiving and communication systems (PACS). The segment also features DeepHealth, which uses AI technology.

In that sense, RDNT’s DeepHealth products are cloud-native imaging informatics with tools that help standardize and provide enhanced reading and management of imaging studies. This technology offers services for cancer screening, allowing for early detection of the disease. These products are also useful for managing risk and follow-ups across population groups systematically. And more importantly, it’s a segment that could benefit greatly from AI as well, which I believe adds compelling optionality to RDNT stock as a whole.

Strongest Quarter, Winning Acquisitions

In fact, RDNT’s services are already showing a rapidly increasing utilization rate because payers and patients prefer lower-cost outpatient imaging. This is a much more affordable alternative relative to traditional hospital-based imaging methods. Plus, RDNT captures demand that moves out of hospitals and into more personalized healthcare services. Management stated that Q4 2025 was their strongest in the company’s history, with a record revenue of $547.7 million. The aggregate volume growth was 14.1%, including existing and newly acquired sites, as well as new builds. Similarly, advanced imaging procedural volume grew by 9.6%, showcasing that the core business is healthy as well.

Furthermore, RDNT's advanced imaging services, like MRI, CT, and PET, generated more than 60% of revenue. And out of all scans done, 28.6% corresponded to these higher-value exams. These results benefit from an ongoing industry-wide trend, where doctors order more of these scans due to the advances in imaging technology. RDNT offers updated scanners to increase capacity and replace old equipment, and their AI-powered software helps optimize scheduling, which is a nice differentiator with healthcare providers and patients alike.

Currently, 151 of 418 RDNT’s centers are joint ventures with hospitals. During 2025, RDNT expanded its joint venture partnerships with 7 centers built and other acquisitions. The Digital Health acquisitions were iCAD, See-Mode, and CIMAR, integrated into the DeepHealth portfolio. In January, RDNT acquired 13 centers in Southwest Florida (Radiology Regional), 6 centers in Indiana (Northwest Radiology), and one center in Virginia.

However, recently RDNT completed a more transformative acquisition with Gleamer. This was a Paris-based radiology artificial intelligence company that will be folded into RDNT’s DeepHealth segment. The deal was all-cash with a post-closing milestone, up to a €230 million purchase price. Gleamer is strong in routine imaging, especially X-ray. However, its combined suite also spans MR, CT, mammography, and ultrasound. And more importantly, has a great strategic fit within DeepHealth due to its AI know-how in radiology, which will likely also be integrated into RDNT’s core outpatient imaging services.

This matters because Gleamer’s acquisition brings FDA-approved and CE-marked devices/software. So, RDNT’s software will now include AI capabilities that flag studies with potentially urgent findings, so they are put at the top of the reading list. Also, RDNT’s AI generates a first-pass report that a radiologist reviews, edits, and signs, improving the speed and consistency of the reports. The software is primarily delivered through the cloud as a SaaS model, with customers paying ongoing subscription fees. Going forward, RDNT expects that the subscription revenue run rate related to this SaaS model will be around $30 million. However, I believe that over time, this alone could become a major revenue pillar for them.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, RDNT currently trades at a $5.2 billion market cap. Their 2025 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $767.2 million in cash and equivalents against approximately $1.1 billion in debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). In total, its book value reached $1.4 billion, and I calculate its EV is around $5.5 billion. It’s also worth mentioning that RDNT is projected to generate $2.7 billion in revenues by 2027, which would price it at a reasonable forward EV/S of 2.0. For comparison, its sector’s median forward EV/S is closer to 3.3, so I do believe RDNT may still have room to run at these levels.

Moreover, remember that management’s latest guidance was also remarkably optimistic. They generated $300.2 million in adjusted-EBITDA during 2025, which is a good proxy for cash flow. And in particular, they anticipate that adjusted-EBITDA from their Imaging Center segment will grow another 18%-22% in 2026, which I think clearly shows RDNT will have more than enough cash flows to fund their AI bets and operations. Plus, it would also imply an EV/EBITDA of 18.3. While this is a bit more expensive than its peers’ EV/EBITDA of 14.0, management’s projected adjusted-EBITDA growth rate seems to significantly outpace the sector’s forward EBITDA growth of 9.1%. Thus, I do feel it’s a justified premium valuation for RDNT.

Alternatively, I also calculate that RDNT generated around $85.5 million in positive cash flow during 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And, while this is a more conservative representation of their cash flows at this point, it nonetheless corroborates that RDNT has a successful underlying model and solid balance, which further derisks their investment thesis today.

Having said that, RDNT’s risk profile is now mostly centered around how well management executes its ongoing strategies. For example, if their revenue growth fails to materialize, or maybe the Gleamer acquisition takes longer to fully integrate, then we could see some multiple contraction for the stock. Similarly, RDNT’s AI bets may not pay off as management expects, and this is not entirely out of the picture since AI is an extremely rapidly evolving and shifting trend. In my view, any of these types of mishaps could prompt an unfavorable repricing of the stock, but I nevertheless remain optimistic since management has delivered on its promises consistently so far.

Conclusion: Great Risk-Reward

Overall, RDNT strikes me as the type of company you want to buy and hold for the long run. It’s a successful business with a healthy balance sheet and rapidly growing cash flows that’s betting on major secular trends. The stock is now also 28% down from its 2025 highs, and the valuation multiples seem compelling when you consider its growth prospects into 2026 and beyond. Naturally, there are always unforeseen events that could derail this bull case, especially since a lot of RDNT’s growth strategy hinges on AI, which adds uncertainty to its otherwise stellar prospects. But, on balance, I believe RDNT is a great “Strong Buy” for investors at these levels who believe in this management team and outpatient imaging business model.

data.title: EastGroup Properties: A Quiet Compounder Delivering Market-Beating Returns
data.description:
Owning hard assets simply makes sense for those who prize cash flow and easy-to-understand business models. It now matters more than ever, especially as SaaS companies have come under pressure due to concerns around AI disruption.

Geopolitical conflicts also make the case for owning assets that are homegrown in the U.S., generating cash flows that aren’t subject to currency translation risk for U.S. investors.

This brings me to EastGroup Properties (EGP), which I last covered in January 2025, highlighting its focus on high-demand, supply-constrained markets and its attractive valuation. It appears my ‘buy’ thesis has paid off, with the stock delivering a 23% total return since my last piece, outperforming the 15% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY). Who says you have to buy popular stocks in the tech sector to get market-beating returns?

In this article, I revisit EGP, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s a ‘Buy’ for conservative income investors seeking long-term capital appreciation, so let’s get started!

Why EGP?

EastGroup Properties is a REIT that’s focused on the development and acquisition of industrial properties in high-growth Sunbelt markets. It specializes in “shallow-bay” business distribution facilities, which are multi-tenant buildings typically ranging from roughly 20K to 100K square feet. Its properties serve last-mile logistics, light manufacturing, and service businesses.

Shallow bay properties tend to have better operating fundamentals compared to big box warehouses. This is due to the fungible nature of the properties that cater to a wider variety of tenants.
