This has also enabled EGP to sustain high occupancy and consistent FFO growth over time. As shown below, shallow bay vacancy has been on average lower than big box properties over the past 17 years.

EGP demonstrated solid Q4 2025 results, with FFO per share rising by 8.8% YoY for the quarter and by 7.7% for the full year. This marks over 10 years of FFO per share growth, signaling the durability of EGP’s business model across business cycles. Occupancy remained strong at 96.2%, marking a 40 basis point improvement on a sequential basis. Notably, same-store occupancy was higher at 97.4%, while newer developments are still in the lease-up stage.

Importantly, EGP is seeing robust rent growth, with re-leasing spreads reaching 19% and 35% on a cash and GAAP basis, respectively, during the quarter. This contributed to strong full-year results, with re-leasing spreads of 25% and 40% on a cash and GAAP basis, respectively. This contributed to strong same-store NOI growth of 8.4% and 6.7% for Q4’25 and the full year.

Looking ahead, development remains the cornerstone of EGP’s growth strategy due to the healthy spread between development yields and acquisition cap rates. Currently, the spread is around 190 basis points, with development yields sitting above 7% and acquisition yields in the low to mid-5% cap rate range. EGP is expecting $250 million in development starts this year, with projects being largely driven by tenant demand within existing industrial parks.

The pipeline of potential tenants is also encouraging, with EGP in discussion with multiple prospects. Notably, tenant demand is coming from a mix of sources, including relocations from higher-cost markets like California. At the same time, new supply coming online is expected to be the lowest since 2018, as shown below.

This is due to limited new development space, where zoning restrictions and permitting challenges are making it harder for developers to bring new projects online. EGP, on the other hand, has a substantial land bank covering 1,000 acres with permits already in place in many cases. This allows it to quickly accelerate projects in response to tenant demand ahead of the competition.

EGP carries a fortress balance sheet with a low debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3x. Its debt to total capitalization sits at only 15%, and it has a very high fixed charge coverage ratio of over 15x. EGP currently yields a respectable 3.3%. The dividend is well protected by a 67% payout ratio and has a 5-year CAGR of 14%.

Admittedly, EGP is no longer cheap at the current price of $188 with a forward P/FFO of 19.7. However, it’s far from being expensive, either, relative to its recent and forward growth outlook.

Given its strong re-leasing rates and development pipeline, I expect EGP to be able to deliver 7-9% annual FFO/share growth in the medium term. This, combined with the dividend, offers investors potential total returns in the low teens, all with a far higher yield and dividend growth rate than the market average.

Risks to EGP include development risks such as cost overruns or construction delays, which could lower the initial yield on these projects. Slower than expected leasing on these projects could also lower the forward growth outlook. Plus, macroeconomic pressures stemming from higher gas prices could result in an industrial slowdown and lower tenant demand in the near term.

Investor Takeaway

EastGroup Properties remains a solid option for investors seeking dependable income and steady long-term growth from a straightforward business model. Its focus on shallow-bay industrial properties in high-growth Sunbelt markets continues to produce strong operating results. This includes high occupancy, robust rent spreads, and more than a decade of consistent FFO per-share growth.

EGP is positioned to benefit from limited new supply, a sizable and permitted land bank, and an attractive spread between development yields and acquisition cap rates.

The stock is no longer deeply discounted. However, EGP’s fortress balance sheet, well-covered 3.3% yield, and visible development pipeline support the case for low-teens total return potential. With valuation sitting higher now than my prior visit, I’m downgrading the stock from a ‘Strong Buy’ to a ‘Buy.’

data.title: Kaspi.kz: Kazakhstan Relies On E-Commerce
data.description:
Although my first coverage of Kaspi.kz (KSPI) is just about a month old, recent Q4 earnings had a significant impact on my view of the company. My original thesis was that Kaspi's growth opportunity is not solely dependent on the success of their expansion in Turkey, with plenty of room for growth in Kazakhstan, especially in their marketplace segment.

The market had mixed feelings about Q4, reacting with +10% on Earnings Day but immediately dropping 6% the following day. Since one of my determined growth drivers, namely m-commerce, was assessed wrong in my initial article, I want to clarify what is driving growth for Kaspi in the future and whether this changes the entire narrative.

Overview & Risks

Kaspi is a super app operating in Kazakhstan. Over 75% of the Kazakh population engage with the app at least once a month, with the average user engaging 77 times a month. The business is divided into 3 segments: Payments enables users to pay their monthly bills or businesses to send money B2B. Marketplace is the main growth driver and allows users to buy goods ranging from cars to groceries or even their vacations online, with same- or next-day delivery most of the time. Fintech is a way to store your money and lend or borrow it at any time, comparable to a typical bank. Additionally, the app offers government services like birth certificates or registering your car, but since there is no revenue to be gained from these services, they will not be discussed in more detail.

With a P/E ratio of 6.7x, it's clear that Kaspi faces risks that could harm the business in the long term. Kazakhstan is a resource-rich, underdeveloped country that relies heavily on oil and gas exports, which account for around 15% of its GDP. Declining oil and gas prices limit the country's growth and Kaspi's growth. Fewer U.S. dollars are being imported to Kazakhstan, which weakens the tenge and fuels inflation. Since 2016, the tenge has lost 27% of its value compared to the USD. U.S. investors should be aware that this directly impacts yield and will continue to do so. Inflation, which is not necessarily due solely to oil price changes, generally demands higher interest rates and cuts spending within a country. Kaspi's outlook for 2025 is affected by high-interest rates, which reduce net income growth by 4%.

Q4 Results

For the most part, Q4 results went according to guidance. Dividend payments returned earlier than expected at 850 KZT per share. With an exchange rate of 496 KZT/USD and a stock price of $73, this implies a dividend yield of around 9.5%, if dividends are upheld for the entire year. Regarding the business growth, payments TPV were up 19% YoY, meeting the upper end of guidance, which ranged between 15%-20%. On a flat YoY take rate, this growth came through more transactions, led by 7% more customers than the previous year. The fintech segment slightly missed guidance of around 15%, with TFV growing just 13%. Nevertheless, I believe these to be quite impressive numbers, since fintech is heavily influenced by the marketplace and suffered throughout the year due to high-interest rates. Both customer savings and loans grew by 18% and 31%, respectively. The NPL rate grew by 0.7%, while the coverage rate shrank by 10%. Although this might sound concerning, management explained that NPL grew as a result of Kaspi getting better at collecting and therefore letting NPLs stay longer on the balance sheet before writing them off. Coverage rate decreased, since loans are increasingly collateralized by cars, demanding less coverage. Due to higher interest rates and higher reserve requirements, fintech net income grew by only 4%, despite 20% revenue growth.

On the Hepsiburada side, management clarified their plan and approach to growing in Turkey. A comparison of Kaspi and Hepsi reveals that the fundamental difference between the two lies in recurring customers. This KPI is defined as consumers who order for 3 months straight. 66% of Kaspi consumers are recurring, while only 29% of Hepsi consumers are. To increase engagement, Hepsi expanded on their competitive advantage of same- or next-day delivery, increasing from 47% to 63% of total orders. This led to a 19% Q4 YoY increase in the number of purchases and an inflation adjusted 13% increase in Q4 GMV. Since the deal only closed in March, followed by the business suffering under a consumer boycott, comparing full-year numbers is not as meaningful.

Marketplace's one-sided growth pillar

In my first coverage of Kaspi, I determined e-commerce and m-commerce to still have significant room for growth and used the graphic below to show that only a third of Kaspi consumers are making use of m-commerce. I argued that, since m-commerce is accessible anywhere in Kazakhstan, the penetration potential is at around 85%–95%.

The fourth quarter showed, that this thesis was wrong. Through e-commerce solutions like next- or same-day delivery and e-grocery, Kaspi management itself is trying to migrate as many m-commerce customers and merchants to their e-commerce segment as possible. This way, consumers receive their needs in a much more convenient way and do not have to drive to the next physical store, and merchants enjoy much more visibility. For Kaspi, customers and merchants in e-commerce have much higher Customer Lifetime Value, since there are more ways to offer more products to customers and merchants, such as advertisement, rather than taking a fixed rate of payment volume, such as in m-commerce. Certain businesses, that rely on a physical experience, such as restaurants, beauty salons, and gyms, cannot migrate to e-commerce. So rather than everyone using m-commerce and e-commerce, we are most likely to see just higher e-commerce penetration and higher revenue per user or merchant. Growth in m-commerce is dependent on the growth of those physically reliant stores.

This story is backed by numbers. While e-commerce and m-commerce penetrations were at 43% and 33% of active consumers in 2024 respectively, e-commerce increased to 47%, while m-commerce shrank to only 30%. Year-on-year, m-commerce purchases were down 1%. GMV grew by 7% YoY on flat take rates, but shrunk 4% in the fourth quarter alone. Meanwhile, e-commerce purchases grew 83% YoY, increasing GMV by 16% on 1% higher take rates. Note that all growth rates are including the smartphone regulations. Excluding this factor results in 27% e-commerce and 11% m-commerce GMV growth. These regulatory smartphone issues do also explain why the high increase in purchases on the e-commerce side does not convert into the same GMV growth, since smartphones are accounting for much higher volume per order.

The entire marketplace segment grew revenue by 23% YoY. Adjusting for smartphone issues lets the growth rate increase to 30%. Net income only grew by 6%, impacted by new tax regulations and extracting worse margins from low-volume orders. Excluding these factors, net income would have grown by 14%.

Outlook for 2026

For 2026, growth rates regarding the marketplace will be around the same as they were in 2025. While smartphone issues are here to stay, the comparison to last year will also include the same issues, making a comparison more favorable. Interest rates, according to management, are potentially decreasing again but are not accounted for in their expectations for the year. Fintech is sort of dependent on the marketplace, since especially cars sold will often result in taking out a loan through fintech. Higher growth rates in the marketplace and lower interest rates could therefore play out in favor of fintech. Management is confident to keep dividend payments up while having enough capital to grow Hepsi, stating:

Clearly, we've gone within an amount we believe will be sustainable going forward. We're not looking to cut the dividend the next quarter.

For the first time, Outlook now includes Turkey. Management guided for 20% marketplace GMV, 15% TPV and 5% TFV growth. Note that these expectations are not considering lower interest rates, explaining only small growth expectations on the fintech side. If take rates are relatively consistent, this should translate into similar revenue growth rates. Additionally, Hepsiburada is expected to be around EBITDA breakeven in 2026.

Updated Valuation

My initial valuation included wrong assumptions regarding m-commerce, making the model hardly useful. Although 2026 guidance metrics for profitability changed from net income to adjusted EBITDA, arriving at net income is traceable, which is why I will keep using net income in my valuation. I will still leave out Turkey for reasons of conservatism, although huge increases in engaged customers are most definitely worth noting.

After initially stating, that I believe saturation of m-commerce to be at 85%-90%, I now assume m-commerce to stay flat at 30%, decreasing potential revenue mid-term to 1.4 billion KZT by 2028. Therefore, my new implied share price lies at around $92, decreasing the margin of safety from 30% to now 20%. My USD/KZT exchange rate also adds a layer of conservatism for these assumptions, since the current rate lies at 494 instead of 549, which would be in favor of Kaspi's valuation.

Conclusion

The return of dividends paired with strong growth when excluding smartphone regulations caused the stock price to increase by 10%, even though there are short-term issues with tax and smartphone regulations. The influence of Kaspi's management has shown in Hepsiburada's results, indicating that retaining and growing existing consumers is of main priority. Although my initial upside was higher, I still believe Kaspi to be an undervalued company at $73, as the fair value, in my opinion, should be at $92 on conservative assumptions.

data.title: IYRI Has Higher Distributions Among REIT ETFs: But At A Cost
data.description:
One of the main drivers of the expected return of the REIT sector we know very well to be the dividend yield. And it is true: in recent years, capital appreciation has really had little impact in absolute terms. And when you hear that there is an ETF in the REIT sector that manages to have an 11% dividend yield (according to SA), your ears immediately perk up. We are talking about the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI), a fund that achieves these results by introducing a covered call strategy that in 2025 beat the benchmark. And the question naturally arises: is this strategy destined to maintain a structural alpha also in 2026 and in the future? Well, in my opinion, even though I greatly appreciate the application of this strategy to the REIT sector, in 2026 things could change. But first …

What is IYRI

IYRI is an actively managed ETF that combines exposure to listed real estate (REIT/U.S. real estate companies) with a systematic call option overlay to transform part of the upside into monthly income.

Two monetization channels: the first, following the composition of the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index benchmark; the second, with a real estate volatility monetization strategy of the covered call type that produces premiums that feed the overall yield.

This in exchange for an expense ratio of 0.68%, high for the REIT category but consistent with the presence of active management + options overlay.

La strategia

The fund invests more than 80% of the portfolio in real estate companies included in the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index. The objective is therefore to create a real estate beta, both on the price side and the dividends of REITs. The second layer, which in IYRI makes the difference, is the options overlay, generally OTM with maturities that today seem to be 10–30 days. In practice it sells call options on ETFs that replicate the benchmark.

Assuming a covered position, an immediate premium is received, which is added to the distributions of the REITs it holds. Income becomes important, premium + dividends, but the sale of the call limits the upside beyond the strike. Specifically, this type of option implies an upside that is still positive (OTM), and continuous rolls (short-term volatility monetization). Then the management can introduce a call spread strategy, which in practical terms is the sale of a call with a lower strike and the buy of a call with a higher strike.

To summarize: it means that the strategy will work well when the underlying rises slowly or remains sideways. Conversely, in strong rallies, the cap on the upside would become a penalty, despite the collection of premiums.

Holding distribution

The index generally includes 60-70 constituents, with an annual rebalance (September) with quarterly reviews (December, March, June), and applies a 10% cap per single security. In fact, the equity exposure is obtained through direct investments in U.S. real estate securities. The first two positions (WELL and PLD) reach about ~20% overall, consistent with the index cap logic; the top 10 instead cover over 50%. This signals a strong presence of “infrastructure/digital REITs” in the top holdings.

Distribution

Distribution rate around ~10.9% with a 30-day SEC yield of ~3.05–3.15%; therefore, a relevant share of the payout does not come from traditional “economic income”, but from options and/or fiscal components such as ROC.

Certainly, on the negative side, the strategy limits participation in the upside beyond the strikes of the calls sold. This is precisely because part of the potential upside is converted into current income.

Peer

Here I could introduce a parallel with other covered call ETFs, many of which I have extensively covered in other research, such as JEPI, JEPQ, SPYI, QQQI and QYLD. But the truth is that although with IYRI they share the call selling strategy, none of these is representative of IYRI’s underlying, which is the REIT segment.

In this sense, the comparison becomes much more interesting by introducing the two most famous ETFs in the Real Estate space, which instead do not implement a covered call strategy: VNQ and XLRE. Good, why do it? Because from the comparison a very interesting piece of evidence emerges. The fund, in 2025, recorded a positive alpha compared to non-covered call solutions in the real estate segment.

The total return of IYRI has been higher compared to that of classic ETFs. The reason lies first of all in the distributions, which, if we think about it, are a bit the core of the return of a REIT. And then there's the fact that in 2025 the performance of the sector has been almost sideways, an element that naturally plays in favor of buy/write strategies.

Not by chance, in the recent period, since the market started the sector rotation that I have extensively discussed in my latest research, the gap in performance seems to be closing, precisely in favor of the solutions that manage to monetize 100% of the upside (VNQ and XLRE).

My opinion

I expect REITs to be range-bound in 2026, in line with the capital rotation process that is increasingly amplifying. The market rewards “value” and “predictability” in returns, two elements that, in my opinion, it finds in the sectors neglected during the last five years of growth, such as cyclicals, and the ex. US segment. In the U.S., the representation of these characteristics, in my opinion, is the REIT sector. Looking more specifically at the benchmark of IYRI, the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index, this evidence can be noticed. The best metric to evaluate would be P/FFO or P/AFFO;

Since there is no synthetic measure of this type here, in my opinion it may make sense to take these metrics as a reference.

Here, in my opinion, EV/EBITDA is the best aggregate proxy where the simple average is ≈ 27x EV/EBITDA distorted by WELL (60x), in fact, without the outlier, ≈ 23–24x. Considering that it is rich in segments linked to the tech sector, in my opinion a coherent growth premium is being paid. In the same way, the average Long-Term Debt/Total Capital is about 50%, also here perfectly coherent with the REIT category.

Therefore, in a context of sector rotation, the REIT sector could potentially have room to expand prices, even without necessarily expanding EBITDA.

Risk

But IYRI does not simply replicate the index: it uses a covered call strategy directly on top of the index. This means that if the market strongly demands the securities within the underlying, price becomes an important return driver, which IYRI cannot mathematically manage to fully capture because of the cap on the upside. Its strength is not price appreciation but the monetization of volatility. This means that the alpha compared to solutions like VNQ could slowly erode; it must be remembered that it is not a structural alpha, but one due to the economic context in which IYRI has been inserted since inception.

And this is a risk in the sense that if the driver of returns of the REIT sector were price expansion and not dividend expansion, ETFs like IYRI would struggle to capture this growth, even though the distributions would presumably remain more competitive thanks to the premium of the options strategy.

Conclusion

Having said that, I think the right rating for IYRI is HOLD. I very much appreciate the application of the covered call strategy to the REIT sector. This increases the distribution, an element that is typically central in the expected returns of this sector. However, the situation in which the market finds itself today seems to be atypical: one of the drivers of expected returns, in my opinion, will return to being price. And IYRI today has a cap on the upside due to the covered call strategy.
